text.tspan: Non-equillibrium Ionization Analysis of a Coronal Shock
Driven by a Coronal Mass Ejection
John Waczak1, John Raymond2, Chengcai Shen2, Jakub Prchlik2
1Oregon State University, 2Harvard-Smithsonian Center For Astrophysics
Coronal shocks are MHD waves in the coronal plasma characterized by an
abrupt change in temperature and density that propagates through space.
Such shocks are often associated with explosive solar events such as
coronal mass ejections and type II radiobursts. At small heights above the
solar surface, they can be seen in the EUV spectrum. These shocks travel
at incredible speeds and energize particles that can subsequently damage
satellites and even affect the electrical grid.
In this study we present a non-equilibrium ionization analysis of a coronal
shock driven by a coronal mass ejection (CME) observed on June 13, 2010.
Using the Solar Dynamics Observatory Atmospheric Imaging Assembly
(SDO/AIA), observations of the CME were made in the 171, 193, 211, and
335Å channels with a corresponding spherical shock present in the 193
and 211Å channels. We constrain previously reported pre and post shock
electron temperatures of 1.8 and 2.8 MK at various positions along the shock
using a fast FORTRAN program to solve the time independent ionization
equations for abundant elements in the coronal plasma. After finishing the
temperature and density fits, we plan to apply this method to multiple points
across the shock front.
Figure 1:
An image from the 193Å channel which
illustrates the CME and associated shock.
The colors have been inverted so that dark
regions correspond to areas of high
intensity and a radial filter has been applied
to enhance features in the solar corona.

tspan.tspan: The round shock can be seen moving

text.tspan: Shock Front
Shock Front
t1
t2
Observed Columns Of Plasma
We created a code to apply non-equilibrium ionization analysis to
coronal shocks. This code enables the determination of pre and
post electron temperatures and densities. Preliminary results
suggest a pre-shock temperature below 1.2MK and a post shock
temperature above 3.0MK. A pre shock electron number density
around 3.5E7 cm-3 appears to acheive appropriate ionization time
scales.
Now that the code is working, we intend to fit the observational data
for the June 13, 2010 shock by performing simulations over a grid of
temperature and density values and minimizing the difference from
the observed intensities in the small observation window. Future work
will repeat this process for windows over a range of positions along the
shock front to analyze how these temperatures and densities depend on
the shock's orientation with the surrounding magnetic field.

Observation (cont'd)

tspan.tspan: Normalized intensity along a thin slice plotted at succesive

text.tspan: times for five AIA channels. The CME appears clearly in each channel.
A separate shock can be seen in the 193 and 211Å channels.
On June 13th, 2010 a coronal mass ejection was observed by the SDO AIA in the
171, 193, 211, and 335Å channels with an associated round disturbance observed
in the 193 and 211Å channels. A previous anlaysis characterized the disturbance as
a coronal shock with a density compression ratio of roughly 1.56 and a shock speed
of around 600 km s-1. Using this information, a post-shock temperature of 2.8 MK
was estimated by assuming equilibrium ionization conditions.1
Figure 3: (up) A small window is constructed to analyze the shock in which the
intensity is tracked as a function of time. (right) The scaled intensities for each
channel are plotted. The shock is observed to reach the window when the 171Å
channel begins to dip. The CME then reaches the window at the peak of 335Å
emission. All analysis was performed between these times.
Figure 4: Example output of a non-equilib-
rium ionization (NEI) simulation for the
ionization states of iron. The blue curve
shows the initial fractions. The red shows
the equilibrium calculation. Grey curves
indicate NEI output at successive times.
The NEI fractions do not approach the
equilibrium ionization fractions even after
To fit the observational data, we employ a non-equilibrium ionization method to
model the ionization processes in the coronal plasma.2 We treate the shock as
a step function in temperature and density with the pre and post shock
temperatures/densities given as inputs. The ionic fractions are then solved for
as a function of time. This amounts to solving
AIA images are 2 dimensional projec-
tions of 3 dimensional phenomena.
Here we show plasma along the line
of sight in the viewing window (see
figure 3) at two times t1 < t2. At t1 the
shock has just crested through the
column and ionizes plasma in the
center (yellow). Plasma at the front
and rear of the colum remains in its
pre-shock state (black). At t2, the
edges of the column are exposed to
the shock and begin to ionize while
plasma in the center has ionized
further to a new state (white).

Figure 6: Example fit for a simulation run with T0 = 1.3E6 K, T1 = 3.00E6 K, and
ne = 7.94E7. We can see that the initial temperature is too high as there is very
little emission in the 171Å channel corresponding to Fe IX. Low counts in the
335Å channel suggest a higher final temperature is needed.
